title: Cogeneration System Flow
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive flow diagram comparing conventional separate generation (56% efficient) with combined heat and power cogeneration (85% efficient)

learningObjectives: Trace energy flow through a cogeneration system and compare total efficiency to conventional separate generation
Explain why waste heat recovery is the key advantage of combined heat and power systems
Evaluate cogeneration as a strategy for reducing fossil fuel consumption and emissions
prerequisites: Understanding of energy forms (thermal, electrical, chemical)
Basic concept of efficiency as useful output divided by total input

keyQuestions: What happens to the waste heat in a conventional power plant?
How does cogeneration capture energy that would otherwise be wasted?
Why is cogeneration not used in every building?
How much fuel could be saved by converting conventional plants to CHP?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think cogeneration creates more energy, when it actually captures waste heat that conventional systems discard
Many students assume all power plants are equally efficient, not realizing that two-thirds of fuel energy is typically wasted as heat
Students may confuse cogeneration with renewable energy -- CHP still burns fuel but uses it more efficiently
assessmentOpportunities: Students calculate and compare efficiency of both systems from the energy flow data
Students explain why cogeneration is well-suited for specific facility types
Students research a real CHP facility and estimate its CO2 savings
